message.content:
Independent verifier for GitHub issue #23 in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch main). You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. Run repros with /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git, no gh.

FINDING (#23, stated MEDIUM): gates.py Gate.evaluate validates legal_transition(self.frm, self.to, self.modes) against the gate's OWN self-declared modes (default ALL_MODES), never the running study's real modes. A CONFIRM→ADJUDICATE gate with default modes approves the transition even for a discovery-only study. The #9 mode-confinement degrades to a naming convention at the gate. Repro: such a gate's evaluate({}) returns approved=True.

Verify by reading gates.py and phases.py. Consider counter-arguments: is it the gate author's responsibility to declare modes correctly (i.e. by-design)? Does any orchestration layer pass study modes yet (it's "wired later")? Is this a real gap now or premature? Decide and assess MEDIUM.

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high|medium|low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Locate phases.py and list repo root

content.content:
1	"""Phase machine — the ordered stages a study moves through.
2	
3	The ordering encodes the method: ingest and build the *blind* baseline before any external
4	claim is touched (Firewall A), and discover before confirm (Firewall B). Gates guard the
5	transitions between these phases.
6	
7	Mode coupling (audit pass 1, issue #9): the legal phase sequence depends on the study's
8	modes. A discovery-only study runs INGEST→…→CONFIRM→REPORT and must *not* enter the
9	external-claims phases (ADJUDICATE, SCORE); an adjudication study includes them. Use
10	:func:`required_phases` / :func:`legal_transition` for mode-aware legality;
11	:meth:`Phase.can_advance_to` is the mode-agnostic full-pipeline adjacency check.
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	from enum import Enum
17	
18	from assay_engine.contracts.study import StudyMode
19	
20	
21	class Phase(Enum):
22	    """Ordered analysis phases. Value is the sort key used to forbid backward transitions."""
23	
24	    INGEST = 1            # raw source → canonical corpus
25	    BASELINE = 2          # build the independent baseline, blind to any claims (Firewall A)
26	    DISCOVERY = 3         # data surfaces candidate hypotheses
27	    PREREGISTER = 4       # lock + timestamp hypotheses before confirmation
28	    CONFIRM = 5           # confirmatory tests on held-out / null distributions (Firewall B)
29	    ADJUDICATE = 6        # convert external claims to verdicts against the blind baseline
30	    SCORE = 7             # score the external source against the validated baseline
31	    REPORT = 8            # assemble the reproducibility package
32	
33	    def can_advance_to(self, other: "Phase") -> bool:
34	        """Adjacent in the *full* pipeline (mode-agnostic). For mode-aware legality that lets
35	        a discovery-only study skip ADJUDICATE/SCORE, use :func:`legal_transition`."""
36	        return other.value == self.value + 1
37	
38	
39	def required_phases(modes: frozenset[StudyMode]) -> tuple[Phase, ...]:
40	    """The ordered phases a study with ``modes`` actually visits."""
41	    seq = [Phase.INGEST, Phase.BASELINE, Phase.DISCOVERY, Phase.PREREGISTER, Phase.CONFIRM]
42	    if StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS in modes:
43	        seq += [Phase.ADJUDICATE, Phase.SCORE]
44	    seq.append(Phase.REPORT)
45	    return tuple(seq)
46	
47	
48	def legal_transition(frm: Phase, to: Phase, modes: frozenset[StudyMode]) -> bool:
49	    """True iff ``to`` immediately follows ``frm`` in the phase sequence for ``modes``.
50	
51	    A discovery-only study therefore legally goes CONFIRM→REPORT and can never enter
52	    ADJUDICATE/SCORE; an adjudication study goes CONFIRM→ADJUDICATE→SCORE→REPORT.
53	    """
54	    seq = required_phases(modes)
55	    try:
56	        i = seq.index(frm)
57	    except ValueError:
58	        return False
59	    return i + 1 < len(seq) and seq[i + 1] == to
60

1	"""Governance gates — deterministic, code-enforced transition checks.
2	
3	A gate guards a transition between phases. It evaluates code-enforced preconditions (the
4	firewalls, lock state) and, where the governance model requires it, a recorded human
5	approval. A gate cannot be silently bypassed: a transition is legal only if the gate returns
6	an ``approved`` decision, and a ``requires_human`` gate refuses to pass unless a provenance
7	recorder is supplied to capture the decision (audit pass 1, issue #11).
8	
9	Current scope: this module implements the precondition check, mode-aware transition legality
10	(issue #9), and the fail-loud provenance/human-approval hook. The append-only provenance
11	*store* and the interactive human-approval UI are wired by the orchestration layer when it
12	lands; until then, a ``requires_human`` gate fails loud rather than silently approving, and
13	the recorder is the seam that store will plug into. The strong present-tense guarantees in
14	GOVERNANCE.md describe that wired end-state.
15	"""
16	
17	from __future__ import annotations
18	
19	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
20	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping
21	
22	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
23	from assay_engine.contracts.study import StudyMode
24	from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase, legal_transition
25	
26	ALL_MODES: frozenset[StudyMode] = frozenset(StudyMode)
27	ProvenanceRecorder = Callable[["GateDecision"], None]
28	
29	
30	class GateError(RuntimeError):
31	    """Raised when a transition is attempted through a gate that did not approve it."""
32	
33	
34	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
35	class GateDecision:
36	    approved: bool
37	    gate: str
38	    reason: str
39	    evidence: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
40	
41	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
42	        object.__setattr__(self, "evidence", freeze_mapping(self.evidence))
43	
44	
45	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
46	class Gate:
47	    """A guard on the transition ``frm -> to``.
48	
49	    ``precondition`` is a pure, code-enforced check over the run context returning a
50	    :class:`GateDecision`. ``modes`` scopes which study modes this gate applies to (default:
51	    the full pipeline). ``requires_human`` marks gates that additionally need a recorded
52	    operator approval, which is enforced fail-loud at :meth:`evaluate`.
53	    """
54	
55	    name: str
56	    frm: Phase
57	    to: Phase
58	    precondition: Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], GateDecision]
59	    requires_human: bool = False
60	    modes: frozenset[StudyMode] = ALL_MODES
61	
62	    def evaluate(
63	        self,
64	        context: Mapping[str, Any],
65	        *,
66	        record: ProvenanceRecorder | None = None,
67	    ) -> GateDecision:
68	        if not legal_transition(self.frm, self.to, self.modes):
69	            raise GateError(
70	                f"gate {self.name!r} declares an illegal transition for its modes: "
71	                f"{self.frm.name} -> {self.to.name}"
72	            )
73	        decision = self.precondition(context)
74	        if not decision.approved:
75	            raise GateError(f"gate {self.name!r} blocked transition: {decision.reason}")
76	        if self.requires_human and record is None:
77	            # Fail loud: a human-in-the-loop gate must not pass without a recorder to
78	            # capture the approval to the (eventual) append-only provenance trail.
79	            raise GateError(
80	                f"gate {self.name!r} requires recorded human approval but no provenance "
81	                "recorder was supplied"
82	            )
83	        if record is not None:
84	            record(decision)
85	        return decision
86

content.text: So there is no orchestration layer that constructs gates against a running study yet — gates are only built in tests. Let me confirm there's no study runner/engine that wires gates to a study's modes, and reproduce the finding.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && ls src/assay_engine/orchestration/ && echo "---STUDY---" && cat src/assay_engine/contracts/study.py
input.description: List orchestration dir and view study contract
Find any study runner using gates

content.content:
__init__.py
__pycache__
gates.py
phases.py
---STUDY---
"""Study definition — the adapter's research questions and pre-registration handle.

A study definition binds an adapter's pieces together for one concrete run: which parser,
which mode, the optional claims source, any extra feature builders, and the research
questions. It lives in the instance repository (one repo per study, ADR-0002), not in the
engine. The engine reads it to drive the run and to anchor pre-registration.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Sequence

from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ExternalClaimsSource
from assay_engine.contracts.features import FeatureBuilder
from assay_engine.contracts.parser import IngestionParser


class StudyMode(Enum):
    """The two modes from the charter. A study may combine both."""

    DISCOVERY = "discovery"
    ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS = "adjudicate_external_claims"


@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class StudyDefinition:
    """Everything the engine needs to run one study, supplied by an adapter."""

    name: str
    modes: frozenset[StudyMode]
    parser: IngestionParser
    research_questions: tuple[str, ...]
    feature_builders: tuple[FeatureBuilder, ...] = ()
    claims_source: ExternalClaimsSource | None = None

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        if not self.name.strip():
            raise ValueError("StudyDefinition.name must be non-empty (it is the registry key)")
        if not self.modes:
            raise ValueError("StudyDefinition must declare at least one mode")
        needs_claims = StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS in self.modes
        if needs_claims and self.claims_source is None:
            raise ValueError(
                "Adjudication mode requires a claims_source; none was provided"
            )
        if not needs_claims and self.claims_source is not None:
            # A discovery-only study must not carry a claims source — it would risk
            # leaking external judgments into a pipeline that is meant to be blind.
            raise ValueError(
                "claims_source supplied but ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS mode not declared"
            )

    @staticmethod
    def discovery(
        name: str,
        parser: IngestionParser,
        research_questions: Sequence[str],
        feature_builders: Sequence[FeatureBuilder] = (),
    ) -> "StudyDefinition":
        return StudyDefinition(
            name=name,
            modes=frozenset({StudyMode.DISCOVERY}),
            parser=parser,
            research_questions=tuple(research_questions),
            feature_builders=tuple(feature_builders),
        )

content.text: Let me check the orchestration `__init__.py` to confirm there's no exported runner, and reproduce the finding.

content.content:
"""Orchestration & governance gates — the analysis graph and its human-in-the-loop gates.

The phase machine advances a study through its structural transitions; deterministic,
code-enforced gates sit at those transitions and cannot be silently bypassed (GOVERNANCE.md).
The graph itself is built on LangGraph/LangChain and lifted from the prior platform.
"""

from assay_engine.orchestration.gates import Gate, GateDecision, GateError
from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase

__all__ = ["Gate", "GateDecision", "GateError", "Phase"]

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
from assay_engine.orchestration.gates import Gate, GateDecision
from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase
from assay_engine.contracts.study import StudyMode

# A CONFIRM->ADJUDICATE gate with DEFAULT modes (=ALL_MODES)
def approve(ctx):
    return GateDecision(approved=True, gate='g', reason='ok')

g = Gate('confirm_to_adjudicate', Phase.CONFIRM, Phase.ADJUDICATE, approve)
print('gate.modes ==', g.modes)

# This study is discovery-only. ADJUDICATE must be forbidden for it.
study_modes = frozenset({StudyMode.DISCOVERY})

# But evaluate validates against gate's OWN modes, not the study's:
d = g.evaluate({})
print('evaluate approved =', d.approved, '  <-- approved despite discovery-only study')

# What the study modes would have said:
from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import legal_transition
print('legal_transition under study modes =', legal_transition(Phase.CONFIRM, Phase.ADJUDICATE, study_modes))
"

content.content:
gate.modes == frozenset({<StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS: 'adjudicate_external_claims'>, <StudyMode.DISCOVERY: 'discovery'>})
evaluate approved = True   <-- approved despite discovery-only study
legal_transition under study modes = False

content.text:
The repro confirms the mechanism. Now let me weigh the counter-arguments carefully.

The finding's mechanical claim is verified: `Gate.evaluate` (gates.py:68) calls `legal_transition(self.frm, self.to, self.modes)` using the gate's own `modes` (defaulting to `ALL_MODES`), and a CONFIRM→ADJUDICATE gate with default modes returns `approved=True` even though `legal_transition(CONFIRM, ADJUDICATE, {DISCOVERY})` is `False`.

Now the key question for severity: is this a real present gap, or by-design / premature?

Counter-arguments to weigh:
1. **By-design author responsibility?** The gate is a static, author-declared object. There is no API surface anywhere for a study's runtime modes to be passed to `evaluate`. The check is structurally a self-consistency check ("does this gate declare a transition that is legal *under the modes it claims to apply to*"), not a study-confinement check. The docstring at gates.py:68-72 even names it "declares an illegal transition for **its** modes" — confirming the author intent is self-validation, not study-confinement.

2. **Is study confinement wired yet?** No. There is no orchestration runner. `evaluate` takes only `context` and `record`; nothing threads `StudyDefinition.modes` through. Gates are constructed only in tests. So #9 confinement at the gate is genuinely not implemented at the gate boundary — but it also isn't *claimed* to be implemented there yet; the module docstring explicitly says the graph/orchestration "lands" later.

3. **Does #9 "degrade to a naming convention"?** Partially fair: `required_phases`/`legal_transition` *do* enforce mode confinement when given the real study modes. The gap is that `evaluate` doesn't receive them. So #9's enforcement primitive exists and works; what's missing is the call site that feeds study modes in — which is the unbuilt orchestration layer.

This is a real latent design weakness: a gate that defaults `modes=ALL_MODES` will silently approve an out-of-mode transition, and nothing forces the gate author to set modes correctly. But it is not currently exploitable because no study-driving code path exists, and the module is explicitly documented as pre-orchestration. The severity is overstated as a present MEDIUM defect.

content.content:
44	
45	# ---- gates (#11) ----
46	
47	def _approve(_ctx):
48	    return GateDecision(approved=True, gate="g", reason="ok")
49	
50	
51	def _block(_ctx):
52	    return GateDecision(approved=False, gate="g", reason="precondition failed")
53	
54	
55	def test_gate_passes_legal_approved_transition():
56	    g = Gate("g", Phase.INGEST, Phase.BASELINE, _approve)
57	    assert g.evaluate({}).approved
58	
59	
60	def test_gate_rejects_illegal_transition_for_its_modes():
61	    # discovery gate declaring a transition into ADJUDICATE is illegal
62	    g = Gate("g", Phase.CONFIRM, Phase.ADJUDICATE, _approve, modes=DISCOVERY)
63	    with pytest.raises(GateError):
64	        g.evaluate({})
65	
66	
67	def test_gate_blocks_on_failed_precondition():
68	    g = Gate("g", Phase.INGEST, Phase.BASELINE, _block)
69	    with pytest.raises(GateError):
70	        g.evaluate({})
71	
72	
73	def test_requires_human_gate_fails_loud_without_recorder():
74	    # issue #11: a human-in-the-loop gate cannot pass without a provenance recorder
75	    g = Gate("g", Phase.INGEST, Phase.BASELINE, _approve, requires_human=True)
76	    with pytest.raises(GateError):
77	        g.evaluate({})
78	
79	
80	def test_requires_human_gate_records_when_recorder_supplied():
81	    recorded = []
82	    g = Gate("g", Phase.INGEST, Phase.BASELINE, _approve, requires_human=True)
83	    decision = g.evaluate({}, record=recorded.append)
84	    assert decision.approved
85	    assert recorded and recorded[0].gate == "g"
86

content.text:
The existing test `test_gate_rejects_illegal_transition_for_its_modes` is itself revealing: it only catches the illegal transition because the test author *manually set* `modes=DISCOVERY` on the gate. The default-ALL_MODES case (the finding's scenario) is exactly the unguarded hole. This confirms the check is self-referential against author-declared modes, with no binding to a study.

VERDICT: PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high

SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: Below the stated MEDIUM as a present defect; closer to LOW now, with a legitimate latent design risk worth tracking against the orchestration milestone. The mechanism is real and verified, but it is not currently exploitable (no code path drives a study through gates), and the gate's mode check is by-design a self-consistency check, not a study-confinement check. Reasonable to log as LOW / design-debt rather than a present MEDIUM bug.

REASONING:
- Mechanical claim VERIFIED. gates.py:68 calls `legal_transition(self.frm, self.to, self.modes)` using the gate's own `modes`, which defaults to `ALL_MODES` (gates.py:26, :60). My repro: a `Gate('confirm_to_adjudicate', Phase.CONFIRM, Phase.ADJUDICATE, approve)` with default modes returns `approved=True` from `evaluate({})`, while `legal_transition(CONFIRM, ADJUDICATE, {DISCOVERY})` returns `False`. So a default-modes CONFIRM→ADJUDICATE gate does approve a transition that a discovery-only study should forbid.
- BUT the framing that this "degrades #9 to a naming convention" overstates it. #9's enforcement primitives (`required_phases`/`legal_transition` in phases.py) work correctly when given the real study modes — the discovery-only sequence genuinely excludes ADJUDICATE/SCORE. The gap is purely that `Gate.evaluate` never *receives* the study's modes; its signature is `(context, *, record)` only (gates.py:62-67). Nothing in the codebase threads `StudyDefinition.modes` into a gate — gates are constructed only in tests, and there is no orchestration runner (orchestration/ contains only gates.py + phases.py; `__init__.py` exports no study runner; study.py has no engine driving gates).
- By-design counter-argument largely holds: the gate is a static author-declared object, and the check is self-validation. The error text itself says the gate "declares an illegal transition for **its** modes" (gates.py:69-71), and the module docstring (gates.py:9-14) plus orchestration/__init__ explicitly state the graph/orchestration layer "lands"/is "wired later." The existing test only catches the illegal case because the author manually passed `modes=DISCOVERY`; the default path is unguarded by design intent, not by oversight in a finished layer.
- Net: a true latent weakness — a gate whose author forgets to narrow `modes` will silently approve out-of-mode transitions, and nothing forces correct declaration — but not a present, exploitable MEDIUM defect. Hence PARTIAL: the mechanism is real (true positive on the code behavior), the impact/severity and "#9 degrades to a naming convention" claim are overstated for the current pre-orchestration state.

FIX_GUIDANCE: When the orchestration layer lands, do not rely on per-gate self-declared `modes` for study confinement. Pass the running `StudyDefinition.modes` into the transition check — e.g. `Gate.evaluate(context, *, study_modes, record=...)` and validate `legal_transition(self.frm, self.to, study_modes)` (intersecting with the gate's declared `modes` if a gate is genuinely mode-scoped). Until then, mitigate the silent-default risk cheaply: drop the `modes=ALL_MODES` default so gate authors must declare scope explicitly, or have the orchestration registry assert each gate's declared `modes` is consistent with the study it's registered for. Add a test for the default-modes CONFIRM→ADJUDICATE case under a discovery-only study to lock in the eventual behavior.
